headline: DNS (Domain Name System)
description: The Domain Name System (DNS) is a hierarchical, distributed database that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `anchorfact.org`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `172.67.x.x`). Designed by Paul Mockapetris in 1983 (RFCs 882/883, superseded by RFCs 1034/1035 in 1987), DNS is one of the oldest Internet protocols still in active use and is a critical dependency for virtually every Internet application. A typical DNS resolution traverses: browser cache → OS cache (stub resolver) → recursive resolver (ISP/public) → root nameserver → TLD nameserver → authoritative nameserver, completing in typically 20-120 milliseconds. DNSSEC (RFCs 4033-4035, 2005) adds cryptographic signatures to prevent spoofing; DoH/DoT (RFCs 8484/7858, 2018) encrypt queries to prevent eavesdropping.

citation.name: RFC 1035 — Domain Names — Implementation and Specification
citation.author: Mockapetris, P.

citation.name: RFC 4033 — DNS Security Introduction and Requirements
citation.author: Arends, R., Austein, R., Larson, M., Massey, D., Rose, S.
